content.method: Instrumental-variable or discontinuity design using classification-threshold cutoffs to isolate the causal effect of SNI status on subsequent school-switching behavior, linked to receiving-school Roma/disadvantaged composition; complement with legal feasibility analysis of comparable admissions-regulation precedents in EU member states with similar constitutional protections for school choice.
content.question: Does SNI/BTMN classification status itself operate as a sorting variable driving parental school mobility under Hungary's free-choice regime, amplifying segregation beyond baseline school-choice effects, and would extending anti-segregation law to free choice be legally and politically sustainable?
content.why_it_matters: T6 is the only proposal addressing relocation of sorting outside the formal SEN system, but it faces the highest legal/political feasibility uncertainty (voucher-financing entrenchment, maintainer autonomy); this determines whether T6 is a viable policy lever or primarily a diagnostic exercise.
